questions.question: How does TD learning split the difference between MC and DP?
options: It averages their outputs

From a single transition (s,a,r,s'), form the one-step target r + γV(s') and nudge V(s) toward it — no model (unlike DP), no waiting for episode end (unlike MC); some bias from bootstrapping, but far lower variance and online updates from step one
It runs DP on sampled models
It eliminates both bias and variance
questions.explanation: The TD error δ = r + γV(s') − V(s) is the online analogue of MC's G_t − V(s_t). This one-step update is the pivot all of modern RL — DQN, A2C, PPO, SAC — turns on.

questions.question: What is the single difference between the Q-learning and SARSA updates?
options: Q-learning uses a bigger learning rate

Q-learning's target uses max_{a'} Q(s',a') — assuming greedy behavior from s' regardless of what the agent actually does (off-policy); SARSA uses Q(s',a') for the action a' ACTUALLY taken next (on-policy)
SARSA doesn't use a reward signal
Q-learning needs a model of P
questions.explanation: That decoupling lets Q-learning learn Q* while exploring ε-greedily — the property Mnih et al. (2015) scaled into DQN on Atari. SARSA converges to Q^π for the running ε-greedy policy.

questions.question: What does the cliff-walking example reveal about the two algorithms?
options: They learn identical paths always

Q-learning learns the optimal cliff-edge path but occasionally falls during exploration; SARSA learns a safer path one step away because it factors exploration noise into its Q-values — more conservative behavior when exploration happens at deployment
SARSA cannot solve cliff-walking
Q-learning refuses to go near cliffs
questions.explanation: Both reach optimal as ε→0, but the on/off-policy distinction has real behavioral consequences while exploring — the practical reason to care which one you deploy.

questions.question: What does Expected SARSA change, and why?
options: It uses the max over actions like Q-learning
It replaces the sampled Q(s',a') with the expectation Σ_{a'} π(a'|s')Q(s',a') — same on-policy target but lower variance since no sample of a' is needed; often the modern textbook default
It removes the discount factor
It waits for episode termination
questions.explanation: Same fixed point as SARSA, less noise per update — a free variance reduction whenever you can enumerate the action distribution.

questions.question: How do n-step TD and TD(λ) interpolate between TD(0) and MC?
options: They don't — the methods are unrelated
Wait n steps of real rewards before bootstrapping: n=1 is TD, n=∞ is MC; TD(λ) averages over all n with geometric weights (1−λ)λ^{n−1} — most deep RL uses n between 3 and 20
By annealing the learning rate
By switching algorithms mid-episode
questions.explanation: The bias-variance dial: more real reward steps before bootstrapping means less bias and more variance. GAE in later lessons is this same idea applied to advantages.
